title: Electrical Interfacing of Nerve Cells and Semiconductor Chips
abstract:
276
¹ WILEY-VCH-Verlag GmbH, 69451 Weinheim, Germany, 2002
1439-4235/02/03/03 $ 17.50+.50/0
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 276 ±284
Electrical Interfacing of Nerve Cells and
Semiconductor Chips
Peter Fromherz*[a]
The electrical interfacing of individual nerve cells and silicon
microstructures is considered, as well as the assembly of elemen-
tary hybrid systems made of neuronal networks and semiconductor
microelectronics. Without electrochemical processes, coupling of
the electron-conducting semiconductor and the ion-conducting
neurons relies on a close contact of cell membrane and oxidised
silicon with a high resistance of the junction and a high
conductance of the attached membrane. Neuronal excitation can
be elicited and recorded from the chip by capacitive contacts and
by field-effect transistors with an open gate. Integrated iono-
electronic system are obtained by the outgrowth of neuronal
networks on the surface of the silicon chip, by implementing
electrical circuits in the chip and by two-way interfacing of the
neuronal and the electronic components.
KEYWORDS:
interfaces
¥
iono-electronic
coupling
¥
neurons
¥
semiconductors ¥ signal transduction
Both brains and computers work electrically. It is an intellectual
and technological challenge to join the two systems directly as
sketched in Figure 1.[1] When we study that problem today, we
are not concerned whether it can be really solved in the
Figure 1. Cartoon of brain ± computer interfacing through the optical and
mechanical pathways screen ± eye and finger ±keyboard, and through hypo-
thetical direct connections of the computer to the visual and motor cortex.[1]
foreseeable future in the sense that brain tissue is integrated in
computers or that semiconductor chips are integrated in brains.
For the present, basic physical questions prevail: Do the
dramatic developments in solid-state physics and neurophysiol-
ogy during the last fifty years permit us to couple electron- and
ion-excitable systems beyond Galvani's twitching of a frog
muscle? How can we overcome the problem of different charge
carriers, electrons in the semiconductor and ions in the neurons?
Can we develop physical concepts for the iono-electronic
coupling and for the dynamics of hybrid neuroelectronic
networks? Whether such investigations will improve our under-
standing of the brain or will lead to applications in medicine and
technology cannot be predicted.
Several years ago, we succeeded in establishing the first
electrical coupling between individual nerve cells and semi-
conductor microstructures in both directions with large neurons
from leeches, and with open transistors and capacitive stim-
ulation spots on silicon chips.[2±4] After these initial steps we
followed two directions:
1. Downwards, we investigated the microscopic nature of the
cell ± semiconductor junction with respect to its structure[5±9]
and its electrical properties.[10±20] The goal is a complete
physical description of the contact in order to optimise
neuroelectronic interfacing, in particular of mammalian cells
(Figure 2).[21±25]
2. Upwards, we started to assemble simple hybrid systems that
combined neurons and neuronal networks with microelec-
tronic semiconductor devices.[26±32] Here the goal is a super-
vision of numerous neurons in a defined net by noninvasive
contacts of the semiconductor substrate as it is necessary for
long-term studies of distributed dynamic processes, such as
learning and memory.
This article summarises our results obtained along these two
directions of research.
Neuron ± Silicon Junctions as Core ± Coat
Conductors
Silicon is suitable as an electronically conductive substrate for
three reasons:
1. A well established semiconductor technology is available to
fabricate microscopic electronic devices;
2. A thermally grown layer of silicon dioxide suppresses electro-
chemical processes that may lead to silicon corrosion and to
cell damage;
3. Silicon chips coated with silicon dioxide are perfect inert
substrates for the culture of nerve cells.
[a] Prof. Dr. P. Fromherz[]
Department of Membrane and Neurophysics
Max Planck Institute of Biochemistry
82152 Martinsried/M¸nchen (Germany)
Fax: (49)89-8578-2822
E-mail: fromherz@biochem.mpg.de
[] Peter Fromherz is a member of the Editorial Advisory Board for ChemPhysChem.
 Interfacing Cells and Chips
BIOPHYSICS SPECIAL
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 276± 284
277
Figure 2. Nerve cell from rat brain (hippocampus) on a silicon chip. Coloured
electron micrograph (scale bar 10 m). The surface of the chip is made of silicon
dioxide. The metal-free gates of a linear array of field-effect transistors are visible
as dark squares. The neuron was cultured for several days in an electrolyte.[19]
Nerve cells, which have a diameter of 10± 100 m, are
surrounded by an electrically insulating membrane. The thin
lipid layer (about 5 nm thick) separates the intracellular electro-
lyte with about 100 mM KCl from the environment with about
100 mM NaCl. The electrical current through the membrane is
mediated by specific protein channels for sodium and potassium
with a conductance of about 10± 100 pS.
Neurons and electrolytes are ion conductors, whereas silicon is
an electron conductor. Upon suppression of Faradayic current
across the interface by silicon dioxide, the electrical coupling of
cells and chips can be achieved only by electrical polarisation.
However, when a nerve cell grows on the solid support, we
cannot expect that the lipid membrane and the silicon dioxide
form a coherent dielectric which would perfectly mediate a
capacitive interaction. Protein molecules, which protrude from
the cell membrane and which are deposited on the
substrate, give rise to a film of electrolyte between cell
and chip, that electrically decouples the cell and chip. A
sandwich structure is formed where a thin conductive
layer is separated from the conductive environments of
silicon and cytoplasm by insulating films of silicon
dioxide and lipid membrane, as illustrated in Figure 3a.
The cell ± semiconductor junction has the physical
nature of a planar electrical core± coat conduc-
tor.[1, 11, 12] Electrical currents and ion diffusion in this
sandwich structure govern the stimulation and the
recording of neuronal activity by a semiconductor chip.
Concepts of Neuron ± Silicon Coupling
The excitation of a nerve cell–an action potential–
consists of a fast opening of sodium channels with a
concomitant current flow into the cell and a delayed
opening of potassium channels with an outward current. These
currents are coupled by the change of voltage across the
membrane which controls the conductances. To describe the
signal transduction between a neuron and a semiconductor, we
approximate the core ± coat conductor of the junction by the
equivalent circuit shown in Figure 3b.[11, 22, 25] The core is
represented by a global Ohmic resistance, the coats of oxide
and membrane by global capacitors. Membrane conductances
also are taken into account due to ion channels, which depend
on the voltage across the membrane.
During an action potential, ionic and capacitive currents flow
through the membrane of the contact. The current is squeezed
through the core resistance and gives rise to a time-dependent
extracellular voltage VJ(t) in the junction. That voltage change
affects the electronic band structure of the semiconductor.[1] It
modulates the current from source to drain of a field-effect
transistor with a metal-free gate oxide, playing the role of a
changing gate voltage.[2] On the other hand, when a changing
voltage is applied to a highly doped region of silicon beneath a
neuron, capacitive current flows through the insulating oxide. It
gives rise to an extracellular voltage transient VJ(t) beneath the
cell which is determined by the capacitance and resistance of the
junction.[1, 3] If sufficiently strong, this extracellular stimulus
affects the cell membrane–in particular it may open voltage-
controlled ion channels–such that eventually an action poten-
tial is elicited.
The efficiency of neuron ± silicon coupling with respect to
stimulation and recording depends on the resistance of the
junction and the current flow through the attached membrane.
Thus, we are faced with three simple questions:
1. What is the width of the gap between chip and cell?
2. What is the electrical resistance of that gap?
3. What kind of ion conductances exist in the contact region of a
cell membrane?
Gap between Cell and Chip: FLIC Microscopy
Silicon reflects visible light and standing modes of the electro-
magnetic field are formed with a node of the electrical field near
Figure 3. Models of a cell ± semiconductor contact. The black lines indicate the silicon
dioxide coat of the chip and the cell membrane. The cross sections are not to scale: The
diameters of the cells are 10 ± 100 m, the distances between membrane and chip are 10 ±
100 nm. Left: AC circuit of planar core ± coat conductor. The area elements of the oxide, of the
membrane and of the electrolyte film in between are represented as infinitesimal capacitors
and Ohmic resistances.[11, 12] Right: Equivalent DC circuit. Oxide, membrane and electrolyte
film are described by global capacitances and resistances. Voltage-dependent ion
conductances are considered in the contact and in the free membrane.[11, 22, 25] VM is the
voltage in the cell and VJ is the extracellular voltage in the junction with respect to the bath
potential.

Gap between Cell and Chip: FLIC Microscopy
Silicon reflects visible light and standing modes of the electro-
magnetic field are formed with a node of the electrical field near
Figure 3. Models of a cell ± semiconductor contact. The black lines indicate the silicon
dioxide coat of the chip and the cell membrane. The cross sections are not to scale: The
diameters of the cells are 10 ± 100 m, the distances between membrane and chip are 10 ±
100 nm. Left: AC circuit of planar core ± coat conductor. The area elements of the oxide, of the
membrane and of the electrolyte film in between are represented as infinitesimal capacitors
and Ohmic resistances.[11, 12] Right: Equivalent DC circuit. Oxide, membrane and electrolyte
film are described by global capacitances and resistances. Voltage-dependent ion
conductances are considered in the contact and in the free membrane.[11, 22, 25] VM is the

CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 276 ±284
the surface. We took advantage of that effect to visualise the
distance of a cell from a silicon chip using fluorescent dye
molecules as electromagnetic antennae in the membrane. This
method is called fluorescence interference contrast (FLIC)
microscopy.[5, 6] We fabricated silicon chips with microscopic
terraces of silicon dioxide (size 2.5  2.5 m2, step height about
20 nm), cultured neuronal cells on that substrate and labelled
their membrane with the cyanine dye DiI. The terraces of known
height together with the gap of unknown width place the
membrane at different positions in the standing light modes
above the surface of silicon. As a consequence, light absorption
and light emission of the dye molecules are modulated.[7]
The fluorescence observed in the attached cell membrane is
shown in Figure 4a for an astrocyte from rat brain on a chip
Figure 4. Fluorescence interference contrast (FLIC) microscopy of cell adhesion.
a) Fluorescence micrograph of the adhesion region of a glia cell from a rat brain
on a silicon chip with quadratic 2.5  2.5 m2 terraces of silicon dioxide (scale bar
10 m). The chip was coated with laminin (3 nm thick in the dry state). The cell
membrane was stained with the cyanine dye DiI.[8] b) Fluorescence intensity
versus height of terraces for the glia cell on laminin (red dots) and for a pure lipid
membrane on polylysine (blue dots).[14] The lines are computed from the
electromagnetic theory of dipole radiation[5, 7] with a water layer between the
oxide and membrane of 109 nm for the glia cell and of 1 nm for the lipid
coated with laminin.[8] The checkerboard pattern corresponded
to the oxide terraces. The homogeneous intensity on each
terrace and the repetitive pattern on all unit cells of 4 4
terraces revealed that a unique distance between membrane
and chip actually existed. The intensity correlated with the
height of the terraces: It was highest on the thinnest oxide, then
dropped and increased again on higher terraces as plotted in
Figure 4b. We also attached a pure lipid membrane on the chip
using polylysine.[14] There the fluorescence started with a
minimum on the thinnest oxide (Figure 4b). The comparison
of the experimental data with the dipole radiation of the dye
molecules showed that the lipid membrane was closely attached
to the silicon dioxide with a gap of about 1 nm. The displace-
ment of the interference pattern for the glia cell was due to a
wide gap between membrane and chip, which corresponded to
a water film of 109 nm. This large distance was caused by
repulsive entropic forces of protein molecules, which were
anchored in the membrane (glycocalix), and were used to coat
the chip (laminin) to promote cellular growth.
We investigated different cell types and various coatings and
could not find cell ± chip contacts that were closer than 40 nm. In
particular we checked whether a closer attachment was
achieved by the cellular structures from focal adhesion. In
fibroblasts cultured on fibronectin, we visualised these focal
contacts by labelling the protein vinculin with green fluorescent
protein (GFP) as depicted in Figure 5a. We performed a FLIC
Figure 5. Focal adhesion of a fibroblast on fibronectin. a) Fluorescence micro-
graph from GFP emission. The elongated focal contacts are labelled with a fusion
protein of GFP and vinculin. b) FLIC micrograph from emission of the dye DiI. The
size of a square is 5  5 m2. c) Enlargement of the GFP picture. d) Colour-coded
map of the distance between cell and chip. There is no close contact in the areas
measurement with the dye DiI (Figure 5b) and computed a
distance map (Figure 5d). A comparison with the GFP pattern of
the same area (Figure 5c) showed that there was no close
adhesion at the focal contacts.[9]
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 276± 284
Probing Junction Resistance by Solid and
We suspected that the gap between a cell and a solid was filled
with electrolyte.[10±12] To measure the sheet resistance of the
core ± coat conductor, we applied an AC voltage, or voltage
pulses, between the cytoplasm and bath[10±12] or between the
silicon and bath[13, 14, 18] and probed the resulting profile of the
electrical potential in the contact region. Two kinds of micro-
1. We observed the voltage profile across the coat of silicon
dioxide using a linear array of open field-effect transistors in
2. We imaged the voltage profile across the coat of the
membrane with a fluorescence microscope using a hemi-
cyanine dye which responded optically to electrical fields.[18]
Here we consider two examples, an AC measurement with a
pure lipid vesicle on a transistor array and a pulse experiment
with a human embryonic kidney (HEK293) cell stained with a
We attached a giant lipid vesicle on a linear array of transistors
as shown in Figure 6a.[14] When an AC voltage was applied
between bath and chip, the voltage on the open gates in the
gap perfectly followed the stimulation of the bath at low
frequencies, as shown in Figure 6b. This coupling is mediated by
Figure 6. Membrane ± silicon junction probed with a transistor array. a) Micro-
graph of giant lipid vesicle on a silicon chip with a linear array of transistors (scale
bar 10 m). The open gate structures are between the ends of the dark lanes of
field oxide that separate the drain contacts. b) Amplitude of AC voltage transfer
(ratio of the voltage amplitude in the gap and of the voltage amplitude in the
bath) versus position and frequency. Above 1 Hz, the transfer is lowered in the
centre of the contact by the capacitive current through the membrane.[14] A sheet
resistance around 100 G of the membane ± chip junction is obtained from the
the Ohmic conductance along the gap. But even at 1 Hz the
voltage transfer dropped in the centre of the junction due to
capacitive current across the membrane and the oxide. By
comparison of the experiment with the theory of a planar core±
coat conductor, we assigned a sheet resistance of about 100 G
to the gap.[14] This value is larger than expected for a gap of 1 nm
filled with bulk electrolyte by two orders of magnitude.
Apparently the density of charge carriers or their mobility is
significantly lowered between membrane and chip.
For the optical measurements we cultured HEK293 cells on a
chip coated with fibronectin. The distance between membrane
and chip was about 50 nm as determined by FLIC microscopy.
We stained the cell membrane with the hemicyanine dye
dibutyl-naphthalene-butylsulfonato-isoquinolinium
This kind of amphiphilic hemicyanine is partially aligned normal
to the membrane;[16] its fluorescence is affected by a changing
voltage across the membrane.[17] We observed the changed
fluorescence of BNBIQ when a voltage pulse was applied to the
oxidised silicon.[18] Exponential optical responses appeared in the
adhesion area with a time constant of about 3 s, as depicted in
Figure 7. By comparison with the theory, we assigned a sheet
Figure 7. Membrane ± silicon junction probed with voltage-sensitive dye. a) Flu-
orescence micrograph of HEK293 cell stained with the dye BNBIQ (scale bar
10 m). The circles mark two areas of the attached membrane (JM) and of the free
membrane (FM). b) Map of time constants of the optical transients after a positive
voltage step applied to silicon. Red colour codes for positive voltage transients
across the membrane (negative optical transients), blue for negative voltage
transients (positive optical transients).[18] A sheet resistance around 10 M for the
membrane ± chip junction is obtained from the measurement.
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 276 ±284
resistance of around 10 M to the gap. Such a low value is
expected when the gap of 50 nm is filled with bulk electrolyte.
The sheet resistance rJ of the core ± coat conductor was used to
assign the resistance RJ rJ/5 and specific conductance gJ 
1/RJAJM (junction area AJM) of the equivalent circuit.
Ion Channels and Electron Channel: Molecular
The membrane of a nerve cell contains voltage-gated ion
channels for sodium and potassium, which open and close
during an action potential. It is crucial for electronical interfacing
when these ion channels are present in the cell ± chip contact.[25]
In initial experiments, we studied the problem with neurons
from a rat brain, as illustrated in Figure 2. We found that one kind
of potassium conductance (K-type) was accumulated and
another (A-type) was depleted in the junction.[19]
A more detailed analysis was achieved in a defined system
made of a recombinant potassium channel on a transistor.[20]
HEK293 cells with a low intrinsic ion conductance were trans-
fected with the recombinant DNA of the human slow poke
potassium channel (hSlo maxi-K). The cells were cultured on a
chip with an array of open transistors (Figure 8a). We enhanced
the voltage VM in the cell step by step using a capillary electrode
fused with the cell membrane (patch ± clamp technique, whole-
cell configuration) and measured the total current through the
cell membrane (Figure 8b). Simultaneously, we observed the
modulation of the source ± drain current in the transistor below
the cell due to the change of the local extracellular voltage VJ,
caused by the ion current through the contact region (Fig-
ure 8b). We found that the intracellular voltage enhanced the
membrane current and that the voltage in the gap rose
proportionally. That result revealed that functional ion channels
were present in the contact region. A calibration with an AC
measurement showed that the recombinant channels were
preferentially expressed in the contact region with a local
conductance of 30 mScm2, an order of magnitude higher than
in the free membrane of the cell.
It is fascinating to combine in a single experiment the two
fundamental methods of our time–gene technology and
semiconductor technology–and to observe the direct interac-
tion of the two fundamental electrical elements of brains and
computers–the ion channels in a cell membrane and the
electron channel in a transistor.
Neuronal Activity and Silicon Chips: Recording
An action potential drives electrical current through the cell
membrane and along the gap between cell membrane and chip.
The extracellular voltage transient VJ(t), which modulates the
band structure of the semiconductor, arises from a superposition
of all capacitive and ionic currents in the contact. The shape and
amplitude of the signal is controlled by the accumulation and
depletion of ion conductances in the membrane and by the
specific conductance of the junction.[25]
In fact we observed a wide spectrum of transistor signals
monitoring the activity of neurons from leeches, snails and rats.
Figure 8. Recombinant ion channels on a transistor. a) HEK293 cells with a hSlo
potassium channel on a silicon chip with a linear array of transistors (scale bar
20 m). The n-type silicon chip appears dark, the p-doped sources (protrusions
pointing upwards) and drains (protrusions pointing downwards) are bright. The
gates (length 1.8 m, width 20 m) show no contrast. With a blue illumination the
transfected cells appear in the colour of the fluorescence of GFP that is used as a
marker. b) Iono-electronic coupling at three intracellular voltages VM  30, 45,
58 mV. Left: transistor response given by the extracellular voltage VJ on the gate
oxide. Right: total current through the cell membrane.[20]
Three classes of signals could be distinguished, as illustrated in
1. The extracellular voltage was proportional to the first
derivative of the action potential.[2, 10, 21] This A-type response
occurred when all ion conductances were depleted in the
contact and the capacitive current dominated.
2. The extracellular voltage was proportional to the action
potential itself.[2, 10, 21] This B-type response was observed
when an unspecific leak conductance prevailed in the contact
such that an Ohmic current through membrane and gap
controlled the extracellular voltage.[10, 11]
3. The extracellular voltage resembled the inverted first deriv-
ative of the action potential.[22] That signal occurred when all
pertinent ion conductances were accumulated in the junc-
tion.[22, 25] A whole class of such C-type signals with variable
waveforms was found for selective accumulation of various
ion channels in neurons from leeches and rats.[22, 24]
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 276± 284
Figure 9. Transistor recording of neuronal excitation in leech neurons. Above:
Nerve cells from the leech on the open gate oxide of a field-effect transistor. Scale
bar 50 m.[11, 21] The n-type silicon chip appears dark, the p-doped sources and
drains are bright. The gates (length 1.8 m, width 20 m) show no contrast. The
cell is impaled with a glass capillary. Below: A-, B- and C-type coupling. The upper
row shows the intracellular voltage VM(t), the lower row the extracellular voltage
VJ(t) on the gate oxide.[21, 22]
The stimulation of a neuron through the solid ± electrolyte
interface without Faradayic current requires a high capacitance
per unit area of the chip beneath the cell in order to inject
sufficient current into the junction. Efficient stimulation spots
were fabricated by high local doping of silicon and insulation
with a thin layer of silicon dioxide.[3] A stimulation chip is shown
in Figure 10a. Neurons from the leech could be stimulated by a
single voltage step applied to a spot beneath a neuron as
illustrated in Figure 10b. The long delay between the stimulus
and the action potential indicated that the threshold is barely
The mechanism of stimulation is not clear. A step stimulus
gives rise to a fast exponential extracellular voltage transient VJ(t)
in the gap between chip and membrane (Figure 7). When the
attached membrane had a high Ohmic conductance (B-type
junction)–as it often occurred with leech neurons–the extra-
cellular voltage transient injected a current pulse into the cell that
led to neuronal excitation. For low Ohmic conductance (A-type
Figure 10. Capacitive stimulation of leech neurons from a silicon chip. a) Leech
neurons on stimulation spots. Radially aligned lanes (60 m wide) are doped with
boron. The chip is insulated with a 1 m thick field oxide. Circular areas (20 ±
50 m diameter) are covered by a thin layer of insulating silicon dioxide. There the
neurons are attached. One neuron is impaled with a micropipette electrode. An
additional electrode (right) measures the local bath potential. b) Excitation.
Bottom: voltage steps applied to the chip with respect to the bath (not on scale).
Top: intracellular voltages.[3] Action potentials are elicited above a threshold of
the step height. The stronger the stimulus, the shorter is the delay time.
junction), excitation was attained only when a burst of voltage
pulses was applied to the chip.[3, 26, 32] Whether a direct gating of
ion channels is involved here remains to be studied in detail.
A physical framework becomes visible for recording and
stimulation of neuronal excitation by silicon chips, though not all
observations are understood yet. We are only in the first stages
of optimisation of neuroelectronic interfaces, on one hand by
semiconductor technology to attain a higher capacitance of the
stimulation spots and a lower noise of the transistors, and on the
other hand by gene technology to incorporate suitable ion
channels in the contact region. Such advances are essential to
efficient interfacing of small mammalian neurons, which couple
Iono-electronics: Neuronal Self-Excitation
A two-way interface of an individual nerve cell was obtained by
fabrication of a stimulation spot and a transistor side-by-side on
a chip.[26] Using that device we built the most elementary iono-
electronic system, the self-excitation of a neuron through a
semiconductor chip.[27] Its function is illustrated in Figure 11a:
1. A neuronal action potential modulated the source ± drain
2. The transistor response was fed into a silicon interneuron
unit, which consisted of three stages: The transistor signal
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 276 ±284
was amplified, identified as a response to an action potential
and transformed to a stimulation pulse of proper shape and
3. The pulse was applied to the stimulation area and elicited
another action potential in the neuron. In an initial approach,
the two-way contact unit and the interneuron unit were
fabricated through hybrid technology with two silicon chips
bonded side by side as illustrated in Figure 11b.
Figure 11. Iono-electronics with neuronal self-excitation through a silicon chip.
a) Circuit. In the upper part the interfacing between neuron and semiconductor is
represented as an equivalent circuit (B-type junction). In the lower part the
interneuron unit of the chips is sketched as a block circuit with the three stages of
amplifier, signal recognition and pulse generator. b) Chamber with chip in a
hybrid technology (scale bar 10 mm). Interface unit and interneuron unit are
fabricated on two separate chips bonded side-by-side. The interface chip forms
the bottom of the circular perspex chamber, the interneuron chip is on its right.[27]
This simple hybrid circuit was an exercise in iono-electronic
engineering. It indicated how neuronal dynamics and micro-
electronic circuitry can be directly integrated at a microscopic
level. As a next step, the coupling of two disconnected nerve
cells through a silicon interneuron is envisaged, that is the
implementation of an electronic neuronal prosthesis at a cellular
Towards Neuronal Nets: Outgrowth and
Having established an electronic interfacing of a single neuron,
we were faced with the problem of assembling neuronal
networks on the chip. If neurons could be joined with defined
connections on a semiconductor chip and supervised by an
electronic substrate, it would become possible to study
experimentally the complete dynamics of neuronal nets. One
of the goals would be the implementation of a symmetrically
connected neuronal network, where the synaptic strengths are
adjusted by correlated stimulations of neuronal activity from the
chip and where the expected associative memory is probed by
recording neuronal activity with the chip (Figure 12a).
Figure 12. Neuroelectronic systems. a) Network of nerve cells (red) with
controlled connections of axons, synapses and dendrites supervised from a
semiconductor chip (blue) by two-way interfacing (black). b) Elementary circuit
silicon ± neuron± neuron± silicon with presynaptic stimulation and recording,
synaptic transmission and postsynaptic recording.[31, 32]
Neurons from invertebrates are the material of choice for
experiments with small neuronal nets. They are large and
therefore easy to handle and form efficient neuroelectronic
junctions. Furthermore small neuronal nets have a biological
function in lower animals. We started to create defined aborisa-
tions of individual leech neurons. Patterns of extracellular matrix
protein from leech ganglia were made by UV photolithogra-
phy.[28] Placing a cell body onto a starting area we observed a
perfect guidance of the growth cone and a perfect bifurcation at
orthogonal branching points of the pattern as illustrated in
There were, however, two problems with chemical guidance:
1. Initially the neurites followed the leading growth cone on the
lane. But in later stages they became shortened at bends and
corners and were disrupted from the guiding pattern as
2. The neurites followed all guiding lanes that were given by a
pattern as illustrated in Figure 13b. There was no control during
outgrowth which could direct the neurite to a certain lane or
that could prevent it from growing in a certain direction.
We were not able to obtain synaptic connections between
grown neurites of leech neurons in culture. Therefore we
isolated individual neurons from the pedal ganglia of pond snails
and cultured them on chips that were incubated with a brain-
conditioned medium.[30, 31] Again, chemical patterns were made
by UV photolithography. We let two neurons grow towards each
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 276± 284
Figure 13. Outgrowth of leech neurons. a) Guided growth and induced bifur-
cation by a chemical pattern with orthogonal branchings. Light micrograph,
scale bar 100 m. The guiding lanes were made by UV photolithography of
extracellular matrix protein.[29] b) Chemical guidance on a hexagonal pattern of
extracellular matrix protein. Electron micrograph, scale bar 50 m. The neurites
grow without control on all given lanes of the pattern. Grown neurites dissociate
other frontally, as illustrated in Figure 14a.[30] After the encounter
of the growth cones we checked the formation of a synapse by
observing the signal transfer by impaled micropipette electro-
des. Negative signals were proportionally transferred as illus-
trated in Figure 14b, a feature that is typical for electrical
Figure 14. Guided formation of electrical synapse between two snail neurons.
a) Micrographs of three stages of outgrowth (scale bar 100 m). Two growth
cones collide on a 20 m wide lane made by UV photolithography of a neuronal
growth factor.[30] b) Electrical coupling. A negative signal in one neuron (blue line
on the left) is transferred to the other neuron (blue line on the right). A positive
current injection into the first neuron elicts a train of action potentials (red line on
the left) that gives rise to an excitation of the other neuron (red line on the
synapses. When a burst of action potentials was elicited in one
neuron, temporal summation of the transferred signals gave rise
to an action potential in the postsynaptic neuron as shown in
In order to achieve a computing neuronal net that is
supervised by a silicon chip, such as sketched in Figure 12a,
we have to solve several problems:
1. The formation of a network has to be controlled during
outgrowth by manipulation of the growth cone;
2. The structure of a grown net has to be mechanically stabilised;
3. Networks with chemical synapses at defined positions have to
The First Step towards Interfacing Neuronal
The first step of interfacing a neuronal net with a semiconductor
chip was an implementation of the elementary neuroelectronic
circuit, depicted in Figure 12b, with electronic stimulation of one
neuron, signal transmission through a synapse and electronic
We fabricated a circular array of two-way contacts made of a
capacitive stimulation spot and a transistor, placed individual
neurons from the pond snail on them and let the neurons join by
random outgrowth in the central area.[31] However, the forces
exerted by the joined neurites displaced the cell bodies from
their contact sites such that an interfacing of two neurons was
observed only very rarely. That problem was overcome by
mechanical fixation of the cell bodies. By photolithography we
fabricated ™picket fences∫of polyimide around each two-way
contact (Figure 15a).[32] These cages immobilised the neuronal
cell bodies (Figure 15b) when a network of neurites formed in
the central area as illustrated in Figure 15c.
Figure 15. Neuronal net on silicon chip. a) Electron micrograph of a two-way
contact with a picket fence of polyimide (scale bar 20 m). Stimulator wings (St)
and transistor (S source, D drain, G gate) are marked. b) Electron micrograph of
an immobilised neuron from the pond snail after three days in culture (after
fixation; scale bar 20 m). c) Micrograph of a neuronal net with the cell bodies
(dark blobs) in picket fences on a double circle of two-way contacts and with
neurites grown in the central area (bright threads) after two days in culture (scale
bar 100 m).[32] Pairs of pickets in the inner circle are fused to barlike structures.
An experiment with a signal loop silicon ± neuron ± neuron ±
silicon is shown in Figure 16. A burst of voltage pulses was
applied to the stimulation area below one neuron, which elicited
an action potential in the neuron. That signal was recorded by an
adjacent transistor. An action potential was elicited in the second
neuron at a distance of about 200 m by temporal summation of
the transmitted signal. The postsynaptic activity was recorded by
an adjacent transistor. In this experiment, interfacing of a
minimal neuronal network and a semiconductor chip was
achieved for the first time.[32]
To attain an electronic supervision of complicated nets with
defined wiring, the technology of interfacing has to be
combined with the technology of controlled neuronal out-
growth. Despite the promising approaches we do not know
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 276 ±284
whether large neuronal nets with hundreds of neurons can be
connected with a defined topology on a chip. An alternative
strategy should be kept in mind: A disordered neuronal net may
grow on a chip with thousands of closely packed interface
structures, such that most neurons of the net are in the range of
a contact site at any time and that the rearranging network is
continuously supervised in its structure and dynamics. The
network may be formed from dissociated nerve cells or in a
It remains open whether the bottom-up approach of neuron ±
semiconductor interfacing leads to systems that are applicable in
neurobiology, medicine or information technology. As basic
research, however, the direction is most stimulating and fruitful.
Pulling down the barriers between very different sciences and
technologies leads again and again to surprising problems and
new insights. The field is certainly most transdisciplinary.
What to do now? With an initial understanding of neuro-
electronic interfacing we are faced with important steps of
optimisation. On the side of the semiconductor we have to
improve the microelectronic structures for recording with
respect to noise and those for stimulation with respect to
capacitance. A wide spectrum of materials may be envisaged. A
transfer of neuron ± chip interfacing to the industrial standard of
complementary metal oxide semiconductor (CMOS) technology
is essential such that chips with thousands of interface structures
and with integrated electronics can be used. On the side of the
nerve cells we have to develop networks with a defined
topology and with chemical synapses, both with invertebrate
and mammalian neurons. The application of molecular biology is
just in its infancy. With that tool we shall be able to control the
mechanical and electrical quality of cell ± semiconductor con-
tacts and also the signal processing in synapses and neurons. We
are confident that our approach will help to
unravel the nature of information processing
In the future we hope that new and
fascinating bio-physico-computational ques-
tions will arise with the availability of involved
integrated neuroelectronic systems. Of course,
visionary dreams of bioelectronic neurocom-
puters and microelectronic neuroprostheses
are unavoidable and exciting, but they should
not obscure the numerous practical prob-
